questions.questionMd: Why is storing data as JSON preferable to storing it as plain text when using `fetch()`?

options.text: JSON is the only format the Fetch API is able to read
JSON files load faster because they are compressed by the browser
JSON eliminates the need to manually parse field separators when deserializing structured data
Plain text requires XMLHttpRequest while JSON works with fetch

questions.explanationMd: JSON provides structured key-value pairs inherently, saving developers from manually splitting plain text strings.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following best describes the "fulfilled" state of a Promise?

options.text: The Promise is waiting for a .then() handler to be attached
The operation encountered a network error
The Promise has been created but not yet started

questions.explanationMd: A 'fulfilled' promise means the underlying asynchronous operation was successful and a value was returned.

questions.questionMd: What is the primary purpose of the Fetch API in a web browser?

options.text: To serialize JavaScript objects into strings
To define callback functions for user interface events
To manipulate the DOM after a page has loaded
To load external resources such as text and JSON asynchronously

questions.explanationMd: Fetch is the modern API for making HTTP requests in browsers, allowing for network resource retrieval.

questions.questionMd: Which function would you use to convert a JavaScript object into a JSON-formatted string?
questions.explanationMd: `JSON.stringify()` converts an object to a string format suitable for transmission.

questions.questionMd: What is the key difference between `navigator.getUserMedia` and `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia`?

options.text: The newer version requires HTTPS while the deprecated version does not
The deprecated version uses Promises; the newer version uses callbacks
Only the deprecated version supports video streaming
The newer version returns a Promise, the deprecated version uses callbacks

questions.explanationMd: Modern web standard APIs frequently utilize Promises for cleaner async flows compared to older callback methods.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, what does it mean to "deserialize" a string?

options.text: To encode a string using HTML entities
To split a string into an array using a separator
To turn a serialized string back into a JavaScript object
To convert a JavaScript object into a JSON string

questions.explanationMd: Deserialization is rebuilding a native object structure from a serialized format.

questions.questionMd: In a chained Promise pattern, what must `onResponse` return for the next `.then()` in the chain to receive the text data?

options.text: A JSON object created with JSON.parse()

questions.explanationMd: To chain asynchronous operations, returning the promise generated by `response.text()` ensures the next `.then()` waits for the text payload.

questions.questionMd: What is the correct syntax for using the real Fetch API with success and error handlers?
questions.explanationMd: A standard Promise `.then()` method accepts up to two arguments: a fulfillment handler and a rejection handler.

questions.questionMd: When chaining `.then()` calls on a fetch Promise, what does the second `.then()` receive as its argument?

options.text: The value returned or resolved by the first .then() handler
The HTTP status code from the first .then()
A new fetch Promise targeting the same resource

questions.explanationMd: Promises pipe their resolution values forward; the return value of one `.then()` becomes the input argument to the next.

questions.questionMd: What does `JSON.parse()` accept as its argument?

options.text: A JavaScript object to be serialized

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.parse()` parses a string that conforms to the JSON specification, producing a JavaScript value or object described by the string.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly rewrites `fetch('f.txt').then(onResponse)` using an intermediate variable?
questions.explanationMd: Storing the promise returned by `fetch()` into a variable `p` allows you to attach handlers to it later using `p.then()`.
JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly describes the old API that `fetch()` replaced for loading browser resources?
questions.explanationMd: Fetch is a modern, Promise-based improvement over the older, callback-based XMLHttpRequest (XHR) API.

questions.questionMd: Why does calling `fetch()` on a local file opened via `file://` protocol result in an error?

options.text: Loading files via the file:// protocol is disallowed by the browser
Local files must be fetched using XMLHttpRequest instead
The fetch API only supports JSON files, not text files
JavaScript blocks all file I/O on the main thread

questions.explanationMd: Due to CORS and security policies, browsers restrict scripts from fetching files loaded directly from the local file system using the `file://` scheme.

questions.questionMd: Who created JSON according to the lecture?
questions.explanationMd: Douglas Crockford popularized and specified the JSON data format.
